text: AI can finish things fast.
Code, research, configuration.
But once the feature ships, can you still explain why you did it that way?
That is why I built Teach Me.
I do not want AI to only do my work.
I want it to help me notice what is worth learning while the work is happening.
If every step becomes a black box, we may ship the feature, but we do not keep the judgment.
A few days later, why this design works or how that bug was found becomes blurry again.
AI can certainly help us walk unfamiliar roads.
We have gaps and blind spots. It can explore, explain, and fill them in.
The problem is not using AI.
The problem is whether we hand over the identity of thinking and creating along with it.
For students or people early in their careers, hands-on practice is already scarce.
Switching tasks, switching jobs, or facing an interview, being able to ask AI is not enough.
You also need judgment, articulation, and review.
Teach Me is not a verbose recorder.
It quietly observes this round of work: coding, configuration, research, or whenever you simply say teach me.
What you changed, what you tested, what error you hit, it collects those clues.
But it never interrupts you.
Only when a stretch of work truly ends does it look back.
Not every trace is worth turning into a note.
It asks: is there a concept here that you should actually master?
If so, it explains the key point in one or two sentences, connects it to your system, instead of piling things up randomly.
Then it updates your knowledge tree, schedules review, and fills the real gap in your skill map.
This loop has four small skills that turn learning into a closed cycle.
Teach Me gathers evidence, explains the key point, and completes the review.
Teach Me Check inspects install status and your learning profile.
Teach Me Recap uses spaced repetition to help you retain what you learned.
Teach Me Exam generates questions from your learning profile to test real mastery.
Knowledge is not yours just because it was written down.
You have learned it when you can retrieve it and use it when needed.
So you can take quizzes right on the site. After choosing, you see the answer and the reason immediately.
All notes are plain Markdown files, living in your own vault.
Local-first by default. Whether to sync with Git is entirely up to you.
You do not have to start from zero.
A PDF, a web page, even an entire Obsidian vault can be brought in.
Teach Me first understands your baseline, then decides whether to archive, explain, or directly quiz you.
Getting started is also fast. Clone the repo, run install dot sh, then install the matching hook for the agent you are using.
In minutes, every work session can leave one more chance to grow.
I love a line: keep building your own brain.
It is not rejecting AI.
It is making sure that when AI frees us, we do not stop being people who think.
Teach Me. Work, and actually learn.
Search teach-me-skill on GitHub. Free, open source, try it now.
